messages.content: Currently I have Point and FPoint, Rect and FRect, LineSegment but not FLineSegment, in SDL it is named LineF, should I keep my previous naming convention with the F in front or use the F in the ending?

In programming, consistency in naming conventions is an essential element of a readable and maintainable code base. When it comes to choosing between `FPoint`, `FRect`, and `FLineSegment` versus `PointF`, `RectF`, and `LineSegmentF`, there are a few considerations:

1. **Consistency**: First and foremost, choose a pattern that is consistent with the existing components of your code. If you have already established a naming convention where types that use floats have an 'F' at the beginning (like `FPoint` and `FRect`), it would be best to continue with this pattern for the sake of consistency.

2. **Readability**: Consider which naming convention feels more natural and easy to understand. Think about how developers will interpret the types when they see them in your codebase.

3. **Convention Alignment**: If your engine is going to be used by developers who might be familiar with other frameworks or libraries, it's often a good idea to align with common industry practices, if it doesn't conflict with your existing code. For example, in Microsoft's .NET framework, 'F' typically comes at the end for floating-point types (e.g., `PointF`, `SizeF`, `RectangleF`).

4. **Framework Integration**: Take into account SDL's naming conventions and how your naming might confuse or aid users who are familiar with SDL's conventions.

Given these considerations, if consistency with your current code is the highest priority and you've already named types with `FPoint` and `FRect`, it makes sense to name your floating-point line segment `FLineSegment` to maintain that consistent pattern.

However, if you feel that having 'F' at the end is clearer or more closely aligns with other frameworks or SDL conventions, then you could consider shifting to that convention for new work and, if necessary, refactoring existing code for consistency (though, of course, that requires additional work and must be carefully managed to prevent breaking changes).

Ultimately, the decision should prioritize clear and consistent naming conventions within your codebase while considering external factors and user expectations. Whatever you choose, ensure it is well-documented, so any developers using your game engine can quickly understand and adopt the convention you've established.

Sure, below are the Doxygen comment blocks for each of the operators you've described for your Rect class:
```cpp
/**
 * @brief Add Point to Rect.
 *
 * This operation moves the rectangle by the offset specified by the point.
 * The x and y values of the point are added to the x and y of the rectangle's
 * position, effectively translating the rectangle.
 *
 * @param rhs The point containing the x and y offset to add.
 * @return A new Rect that represents the translated rectangle.
 */
Rect Rect::operator+(const Point& rhs) const;

/**
 * @brief Subtract Point from Rect.
 *
 * This operation moves the rectangle by the negative offset specified by the point.
 * The x and y values of the point are subtracted from the x and y of the rectangle's
 * position, effectively translating the rectangle.
 *
 * @param rhs The point containing the x and y offset to subtract.
 * @return A new Rect that represents the translated rectangle.
 */
Rect Rect::operator-(const Point& rhs) const;

/**
 * @brief Multiply Rect dimensions by a scalar.
 *
 * This operator scales the rectangle's width and height by the given scalar value.
 * The scaling is uniform and affects both dimensions. The rectangle's position
 * remains unchanged.
 *
 * @param scalar The scalar by which to multiply the dimensions of the rectangle.
 * @return A new Rect that represents the scaled rectangle.
 */
Rect Rect::operator*(const int scalar) const;

/**
 * @brief Add Point to this Rect (compound assignment).
 *
 * This operator moves the rectangle by the offset specified by the point,
 * assigning the new position to the rectangle itself.
 * The x and y values of the point are added to the x and y of the rectangle's
 * position, effectively translating the rectangle.
 *
 * @param rhs The point containing the x and y offset to add.
 * @return A reference to this rectangle after translation.
 */
Rect& Rect::operator+=(const Point& rhs);

/**
 * @brief Subtract Point from this Rect (compound assignment).
 *
 * This operator moves the rectangle by the negative offset specified by the point,
 * assigning the new position to the rectangle itself.
 * The x and y values of the point are subtracted from the x and y of the rectangle's
 * position, effectively translating the rectangle.
 *
 * @param rhs The point containing the x and y offset to subtract.
 * @return A reference to this rectangle after translation.
 */
Rect& Rect::operator-=(const Point& rhs);

/**
 * @brief Multiply the dimensions of this Rect by a scalar (compound assignment).
 *
 * This operator scales the rectangle's width and height by the given scalar value,
 * assigning the new dimensions to the rectangle itself. The scaling is uniform and
 * affects both dimensions. The rectangle's position remains unchanged.
 *
 * @param scalar The scalar by which to multiply the dimensions of the rectangle.
 * @return A reference to this rectangle after scaling.
 */
Rect& Rect::operator*=(const int scalar);
```

These comments provide a clear description of how each operator modifies the rectangle and the effect it has on the rectangle's properties. Make sure to integrate the documentation into your project consistently and keep it up to date with any changes to the functionality or behavior of your classes and methods.

OK found out there are three methods I haven't implemented:

SDL_StartTextInput, SDL_SetTextInputRect and SDL_StopTextInput.

Where do I put these? Where is the proper place? I guess the Start and Stop would be in the Window class but the Set in the Rectangle also makes no sense if the Rectangle doesn't call Start and Stop methods... what do you think?

I already have an InputManager but only handle key events, doesn't deal with Text, so maybe adding this new manager makes sense right? Or should I just wait till I start developing the GUI elements?? If so, would I need this manager if there is a GUI element text field or something?

The decision to integrate text input management now or to wait until you start developing GUI elements will depend on your project's requirements and your development strategy. Here's a breakdown of the considerations:

1. **Add Now to Existing InputManager**:
   - **Pros**:
     - You have a centralized location to handle all input-related functionality, which could simplify the design and avoid scattering input-handling logic across different parts of your engine.
     - If you foresee the need for text input separate from GUI components (for example, for entering console commands or chat messages), having text input logic in the InputManager makes sense.
   - **Cons**:
     - If you're not going to need text input functionality until later, you might be adding complexity and potentially unused code to your engine prematurely.

2. **Wait Until GUI Development**:
   - **Pros**:
     - You delay implementation until you have a clear understanding of the GUI requirements, which can lead to a design that's better tailored to those needs.
     - You can avoid implementing features that might not be used or might need considerable changes once your GUI is in place.
   - **Cons**:
     - You might encounter challenges or design constraints with text input that could have been addressed early on had you integrated text input handling sooner.

3. **Add a Separate GUI Text Input Manager**:
   - **Pros**:
     - If your GUI system is complex or heavily featured, a specialized text input manager for the GUI can encapsulate the nuances of GUI-related text entry.
     - This could allow for more specialized handling of GUI text events, such as text validation or on-screen keyboard support, especially on platforms where this is a consideration.
   - **Cons**:
     - You will have multiple managers handling different types of input, which might reduce code cohesion and make debugging input-related issues more complicated.

The decision also somewhat depends on the scope of your InputManager:

- If your InputManager is designed to handle all types of user input, including keyboard, mouse, gamepad, and potentially touch, it might be logical to also make it handle text input.
- If the InputManager is currently limited to handling discrete key events and not continuous text streams, it may be less disruptive to add text input management functionality to it now.

Given these considerations, if you anticipate needing text input before implementing your GUI system, or want to design your input system with future expansion in mind, it might be a good idea to start integrating text input into your InputManager now. You could refactor it to be more generic to handle different input types, including text input.

However, if text input is inextricably linked with your GUI system and you don't see the need for text input elsewhere, then waiting until you develop your GUI elements and creating a GUI TextInputManager as part of or alongside your GUI system may be the best approach.

If you choose to wait, you will still need some sort of text input management even with GUI text field elements. The logic for starting, stopping, and setting text input areas will have to live somewhere, and a TextInputManager could be a clean way to encapsulate that functionality.

In either case, ensure your design is flexible and adaptable, so you're not cornered if your requirements change as your game engine develops.

In another instance of you, you said to me this about my Renderer class:

In your implementation, make sure you also handle cases like losing the graphics device context and be prepared to recreate the renderer if needed. Proper resource and state management is critical given that the renderer is often central to the performance and functional stability of your game engine.

After I carefully read it I see that I don't handle these cases, how can I do this? Do I really need to do this? What alternatives there is to solve this?
It looks good, but how do I do these Periodic Checks or event handling? Now that I got the methods I need a way to detect if the Renderer is lost or not
